method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
No source documents were provided, so the requested section cannot be written with the required document citations.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants and therefore no resident-based economy.[1] Its principal historical economic resource was guano, and the United States claimed the island in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act, but the deposits are now exhausted.[1] In the 1930s, the island was colonized from Hawaii and developed for use as an aviation stop on routes between Hawaii and Australia, a role associated with Amelia Earhart’s planned 1937 landfall there.[1] Contemporary economic use is highly limited because the island is administered as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and is part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] The surrounding U.S. Pacific Remote Island Areas, which include Howland Island, have been included in regional pelagic fishing activity, but they accounted for only about 2 percent of the cited Hawaiʻi-based longline fishing deployments in U.S. exclusive economic zone areas.[3]

==Time zone==
Howland Island is assigned to UTC−12:00, the same offset as nearby Baker Island and identified in nautical time-zone notation as Y, or “Yankee”.[2] The island is uninhabited, and public entry is permitted only by special permit for U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service purposes.[4][5] Because UTC−12:00 is the last civil time zone in which a calendar date remains in effect, deadline conventions using “Anywhere on Earth” treat the end of the day on Howland and Baker Islands as the final expiration point for a date.[3] The last inhabited places to reach the end of a day are generally in UTC−11:00, such as Niue and American Samoa, while the day technically ends one hour later on uninhabited Howland and Baker Islands.[5] The maximum date-line contrast with parts of Kiribati is 26 hours, so a time in Kiribati can be more than a full calendar day ahead of Howland Island.[1]

==History==
Evidence cited in modern accounts indicates an early Polynesian presence on Howland Island before its 19th-century encounters with American whalers.[4] Although one account attributes the island’s discovery to American sailor Edmund Fanning in 1791, other sources identify the first recorded sighting as occurring in 1822, when Nantucket whaling captain George Worth named it Worth Island.[1][2] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship *Isabella* visited the island and gave it the name Howland Island, reportedly after the ship’s lookout.[2][4]

The United States claimed Howland in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2] Guano extraction began in the late 1850s after rival American companies sought to exploit the island’s deposits, and a court settlement in 1865 allowed both companies to mine there.[4] These guano-mining operations ended in 1878, when the deposits had been depleted.[4] Other summaries note that guano was mined on the island before 1890 by both American and British interests.[3]

In 1935, colonists from the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panala‘au Project, arrived from Hawaii to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The initial group consisted of four male students and alumni from the Kamehameha School in Honolulu, who had been recruited as part of what was described as a scientific expedition to collect biological and botanical samples.[4] The settlement they established was called Itascatown, after the U.S. Coast Guard cutter *Itasca*.[4] Howland was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936, during a period when it was intended to serve as an aviation stop on routes between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

On July 2, 1937, Amelia Earhart and navigator Fred Noonan disappeared near Howland Island while attempting to reach it from Lae, New Guinea, during Earhart’s around-the-world flight.[2] The island later served as a United States military lookout point during World War II.[1] After the wartime and colonization period, Howland’s historical role shifted from settlement and strategic use toward conservation, and it is now managed as part of the United States National Wildlife Refuge system and the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[2][3]

==Earhart Light==
The structure commonly known as Earhart Light is a small lighthouse or day beacon built in 1937, when Howland’s airstrip facilities were being prepared for Amelia Earhart’s planned refueling stop during her round-the-world flight.[3] Although radio transmissions from Earhart were received on Howland, her aircraft never arrived, and the beacon became associated with the landfall she failed to reach.[3]  

The beacon was damaged during Japanese attacks in World War II; accounts describe bombing and shelling that rendered it useless, and a plaque honoring Earhart was shattered by machine-gun fire.[2][5] The light station has been inactive since 1942, and the surviving structure is regarded as a day beacon rather than an operating navigational light.[3][5] The lighthouse was rebuilt in the early 1960s, and a 1962 photograph identifies the structure as the Amelia Earhart light beacon on Howland Island.[2][4] Later visits to the island included restoration work on the beacon and the addition of the names of the two colonists killed in the wartime attack.[2]

==Image gallery==
The gallery commonly emphasizes Howland Island’s isolation, low coral terrain, and surrounding reef, using maps and orbital imagery rather than settlement views because the island is uninhabited and managed as a wildlife refuge.[1][2][4]

A map is often used to identify the island’s position in the central Pacific, southwest of Honolulu and between Hawaii and Australia.[1][2]

NASA astronaut imagery from ISS Expedition 10, photographed on December 3, 2004, shows the island from orbit with its fringing reef visible as white breakers around the shoreline.[2][3][4]

The NASA image metadata identifies the photograph as frame ISS010-E-09287, taken with a Kodak DCS760C digital camera and an 800 mm lens from an altitude of about 192 nautical miles.[2][3][4]

Such high-resolution astronaut photographs are used to update maps of reefs and islands, monitor reef ecosystems, and support bathymetric analysis of the surrounding ocean floor.[2][4]

The imagery also illustrates the island’s role as habitat for nesting, roosting, and foraging seabirds, shorebirds, and marine wildlife.[2][5]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974, encompassing the island and its surrounding waters, including submerged and emergent lands from the mean low-tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge protects a small coral island and active fringing reef that provide nesting habitat and foraging areas for seabirds and other marine wildlife.[1]  

In 2009, the refuge became part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, and its protected refuge waters were extended to 12 nautical miles.[4] The broader monument area around Howland extends to 50 nautical miles and is cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.[4]  

Refuge planning documents prepared under the National Wildlife Refuge System Administration Act, the National Wildlife Refuge System Improvement Act, and the National Environmental Policy Act proposed increased biological surveys, habitat assessment, rare-seabird attraction projects, and expanded scientific research opportunities.[5] The preferred management alternative called for annual monitoring and research activities while maintaining the same general scope and complexity of refuge management.[5] No public recreational use of the refuge was proposed in those planning documents.[5]  

Conservation concerns for Howland and the surrounding monument waters include invasive species, protection of seabird and marine habitats, and the long-term effects of ocean warming and acidification on coral reef ecosystems.[4] Scientific assessments of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument have identified high fish biomass in some of its equatorial and central Pacific areas and notable abundances of Endangered Species Act-listed species, including green sea turtles.[4]
